coredata.dc:title: Oxidized plant leaf-derived carbon dots as novel electrochemiluminescent luminophores for ultrasensitive microRNA-21 detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Gui
Han, Qian
Song, Li
Liu, Pingkun
Kuang, Guangrong
Fu, Yingzi
coredata.dc:description: The enthusiasm of many chemists for green chemistry has increased due to its clean and sustainable features. Here, plant leaf-derived carbon dots (CDPs) and oxidized CDPs (o-CDPs) were synthesized through a simple pyrolytic procedure. The results of the controlled trial showed that the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) performances of o-CDPs were significantly better than those of CDPs. Among them, oxidized maple CDP (o-CDP-M) exhibited the highest ECL intensity with a maximum emission wavelength of 520 nm and could be used as a new type of "green source" luminophore. In view of the considerable role of microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) in tumour overexpression, it was chosen as the target molecule. In combination with the cascade amplification strategy, an ultrasensitive “signal-on” ECL biosensor based on o-CDP-M was developed. Possessing the merits of eco-friendliness and high selectivity, the proposed biosensor achieved ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-21 with a dynamic range from 1 fM to 1 nM and a detection limit as low as 0.27 fM. It was successfully used to analyse miRNA-21 in tumour cell extracts. This work provides a new opportunity for synthesizing “green source” luminophores and detecting various trace miRNA species.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxidized plant leaf-derived carbon dots
MicroRNA-21
Cascade amplification strategy
Electrochemiluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDPs and o-CDPs modified electrode
Preparation of o-CDP-M (or CDP-M) and DNA bioconjugates
ECL properties of CDPs and o-CDPs
Analytical performance of the proposed biosensor
Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the proposed biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22077105 ) and Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC ( cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0854 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, HRTEM (upper inset) images and the size distribution histograms (lower inset) of (A) CDP-M and (B) o-CDP-M. (C) FTIR spectra, (D) Raman spectra, (E) UV–vis absorption spectra and (F) FL emission spectra (λex = 370 nm): (a) CDP-M and (b) o-CDP-M.
XPS spectra of (A) CDP-M and (B) o-CDP-M. C 1s HR-XPS spectra of (C) CDP-M and (D) o-CDP-M. N 1s HR-XPS spectra of (E) CDP-M and (F) o-CDP-M.

ECL intensity-potential curves of (A) CDPs and (B) o-CDPs prepared from different leaves: (a) maple, (b) Ficus virens Aiton, (c) banana and (d) bare GCE. (C) Stability of the ECL signal of o-CDP-M/GCE. (D) The ECL intensity-potential-wavelength 3D surface image of o-CDP-M/GCE. All test solution containing 50 mM S2O8 2− (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4).

The PAGE (16 %) presenting the assembly the amplification strategy: (A) lane 1, miRNA-21; lane 2, H1; lane 3, H2; lane 4, miRNA-21+H1 + H2; lane 5, H3; lane 6, H4; lane 7, miRNA-21+H1 + H2+H3 + H4. (B) lane 1, H1; lane 2, H2; lane 3, H1 + H2; lane 4, H3; lane 5, H4; lane 6, H3 + H4; lane 7, H1 + H2+H3 + H4. (C) CV and (D) EIS characterization for the stepwise biosensor in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4) at the scanning rate of 100 mV/s: (a) bare GCE, (b) Au/GCE, (c) SH-CP/Au/GCE, (d) MCH/SH-CP/Au/GCE, (e) CHA/MCH/SH-CP/Au/GCE, (f) HCR/CHA/MCH/SH-CP/Au/GCE.

ECL intensity-time curves of biosensors based on (A) o-CDP-M and (B) CDP-M towards variable concentrations of miRNA-21: (a) 1 fM, (b) 10 fM, (c) 100 fM, (d) 1 pM, (e) 10 pM, (f) 100 pM, (g) 1 nM; Biosensors based on (C) o-CDP-M and (D) CDP-M: the resulting linear relationship between the ECL intensity and logarithm of miRNA-21 concentration.

(A) Stability of the proposed ECL biosensor under consecutive scans for 16 cycles; (B) Reproducibility and (C) Selectivity of the proposed biosensor; (D) Detection of miRNA-21 from MCF-7 cells and HeLa cells.
Schematic illustration of (A) the formation process of carbon dots and oxidized carbon dots from maple leaves and (B) the ECL biosensor configuration strategy for miRNA-21 detection.

head.ce:title: Oxidized plant leaf-derived carbon dots as novel electrochemiluminescent luminophores for ultrasensitive microRNA-21 detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The enthusiasm of many chemists for green chemistry has increased due to its clean and sustainable features. Here, plant leaf-derived carbon dots (CDPs) and oxidized CDPs (o-CDPs) were synthesized through a simple pyrolytic procedure. The results of the controlled trial showed that the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) performances of o-CDPs were significantly better than those of CDPs. Among them, oxidized maple CDP (o-CDP-M) exhibited the highest ECL intensity with a maximum emission wavelength of 520 nm and could be used as a new type of "green source" luminophore. In view of the considerable role of microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) in tumour overexpression, it was chosen as the target molecule. In combination with the cascade amplification strategy, an ultrasensitive “signal-on” ECL biosensor based on o-CDP-M was developed. Possessing the merits of eco-friendliness and high selectivity, the proposed biosensor achieved ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-21 with a dynamic range from 1 fM to 1 nM and a detection limit as low as 0.27 fM. It was successfully used to analyse miRNA-21 in tumour cell extracts. This work provides a new opportunity for synthesizing “green source” luminophores and detecting various trace miRNA species.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) integrates the obvious strengths of chemiluminescence and electrochemistry, which places a firm foothold in the areas of clinical diagnosis and biosensing [1–5]. Carbon dots (CDs), which have the advantages of ultrasmall size, low cytotoxicity and unique luminescent properties, are hopefully becoming a competitive choice for heavy metal-based quantum dots in ECL. Although research on CDs has made significant progress, some drawbacks are still worth breaking through. On the one hand, CDs formed from the precursors of fine carbon structures (such as graphene and carbon nanotubes) or conventional chemicals are not sufficiently benign [6–8]. In addition, the synthesis pathways containing microwave preparation, hydrothermal synthesis and laser ablation are relatively complicated, which hampers the batch production of CDs. To overcome the above bottlenecks, the use of natural carbon sources to synthesize CDs has attracted attention [9,10]. Leaves, mainly composed of carbohydrates, are safely and easily available renewable biological resources. Plant leaf-derived CDs (CDPs) are green, low-cost and eco-friendly. Mint leaf-based CDs have been prepared for fluorescence detection [11], and ginkgo leaf-based CDs have been well applied for biological imaging [12]. However, little research has been reported on leaf-based CDs in ECL. On the other hand, the relatively low ECL intensity of CDs is also a prominent problem. A variety of channels have been used to improve ECL performance, such as heteroatomic doping and the oxidation of CDs [13].

MicroRNAs (miRNAs), a class of small (approximately 19–23 nucleotides) and endogenous noncoding RNAs, have emerged as a new generation tumour marker [14,15]. Since the content of miRNA-21 is closely related to breast cancer, glioma tumour and oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma [16,17], the miRNA-21 assay has become the focus [18,19]. However, the monitoring of miRNA-21 with higher sensitivity and stability needs to be further improved. A nucleic acid amplification strategy is an effective method to help solve this problem. Catalysed hairpin assembly (CHA), as a target recycling amplification technique, is versatile, isothermal and enzyme-free [20,21]. Hybridization chain reaction (HCR) generates numerous double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) polymers through two alternately complementary hairpin DNAs, which can provide exponential amplification [22–24].

Herein, CDPs and oxidized CDPs (o-CDPs) were synthesized by pyrolysis. The photoelectric properties of CDPs and o-CDPs prepared from three kinds of leaf sources (maple leaves, Ficus virens Aiton leaves, and banana leaves) were contrastively investigated. In addition, combined with the cascade amplification strategy of CHA and HCR, the performances of "signal-on" ECL biosensors by employing o-CDP-M and CDP-M as a new type of “green source” luminophores for the detection of miRNA-21 were comparatively discussed. The proposed ECL biosensor has potential applications in the field of bioanalysis or even clinical diagnosis.

The leaves were picked from the campus of Southwest University. All oligonucleotides were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). The sequences are as follows: the capture probe with a mercaptohexyl group at the 5’-phosphate end (SH-CP, 5’-SH-TTT TTT AGT CTG A-3’), hairpin DNA1 (H1, 5’-TCA ACA TCAGTCTGATAAGCTACT ACA ACT GGC GGG GTAGCTTATCAGACT-3’), H2 (5’-TAA GCT ACCCCGCCAGTTGTAGTA GCT TAT CAG ACT GAT GTCTACAAC TGG CGG GG-3’), H3 (5’-CTG GCG GGGAGG AAG CCC CGC CAGTTG TAGTTT TTT TTT-NH2-3’), H4 (5’-NH2-TTT TTT TTT CTT CCT CCC CGC CAGCTA CAA CTG GCG GGG-3’), miRNA-21 (UAG CUU AUC AGA CUG AUG UUG A), miRNA-155 (UUA AUG CUA AUC GUG AUA GGG GU), miRNA-182-5p (UUU GG CAA UGG UAG AAC UCA CAC U), and miRNA-126 (UCG UAC CGU GAG UAA UAA UGC G). The bold sequences in the same oligonucleotide were complementary and could be annealed to form hairpins. Other reagents and the preparation of buffers, including phosphate-buffered saline (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4) and Tris−HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4) are shown in the supplementary material (SI). RNA was dissolved in diethylpyrocarbonate-treated water, and DNA was dissolved in Tris−HCl. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ⋅cm) was used for the entire experiment.

The ECL measurements were executed on a model MPI-E electrocheminescence analyser (Xi'an Remex Analysis Instruments Co., Ltd., China) using a three-electrode system (bare or modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum electrode) in 50 mM K2S2O8 (as a coreactant, 0.1 M PBS, pH, 7.4). The potential scan was set from 0 to -2 V, and the photomultiplier tube was set at 800 V. Other apparatuses are listed in the SI. The experimental temperature was 25 ± 1 °C.

CDPs were prepared as follows: cleaned leaves (maple, F. virens Aiton, and banana) as precursors were immersed in 0.1 M KCl solution for 12 h, washed alternately with water and ethanol, and dried at room temperature. Next, a quartz boat loaded with leaves was transferred to a tube furnace and pyrolyzed in a nitrogen atmosphere at 350 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the black products were ground into a powder with an agate mortar. First, 0.8 g of the procured sample was dispersed in 8 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 20 min to form a black solution. The large particles were removed from the solution through a filter membrane (0.22 μm pore size). Three kinds of CDPs (denoted as CDP-M, CDP-F and CDP-B) were collected by centrifugation two times at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatants were stored at 4 °C.

Four millilitres of 30% H2O2 was dropped into 4 mL of different CDP solutions, and the mixture was stirred magnetically at 80 °C for 4 h. Then, the solutions were cooled and dialyzed using a dialysis bag (MWCO, 3000 Da) for 24 h to remove excess H2O2. The three obtained oxidized plant leaf-derived carbon dot solutions were denoted o-CDP-M, o-CDP-F and o-CDP-B and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDPs and o-CDPs modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Each GCE was polished with aluminium slurry and washed with ethanol and water in turn. Ten microlitres of o-CDP-M, o-CDP-F, o-CDP-B, CDP-M, CDP-F or CDP-B was dropped on the GCE surface and dried naturally at room temperature (denoted as o-CDP-M/GCE, o-CDP-F/GCE, o-CDP-B/GCE, CDP-M/GCE, CDP-F/GCE or CDP-B/GCE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of o-CDP-M (or CDP-M) and DNA bioconjugates
ce:section.ce:para: Before use, the hairpin DNA was annealed at 95 °C for 5 min and then cooled to room temperature. Initially, 2 μL of 20 mM N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride and 2 μL of 10 mM N-hydroxy succinimide were added to 20 μL of o-CDP-M for activation for 2 h. Next, the mixture of activated o-CDP-M with 20 μL of 10 μM annealed amino-functionalized hairpin DNA (H3 or H4) and 180 μL of Tris−HCl buffer was stirred continuously for 2 h to obtain the bioconjugates of o-CDP-M and DNA (H3 or H4) via amide bonds. After centrifugation and washing at 12,000 rpm for 45 min at 4 °C, the obtained supernatant was o-CDP-M-labelled H3 or o-CDP-M-labelled H4 (o-CDP-M-H3 or o-CDP-M-H4) and stored at 4 °C when not in use. CDP-M and DNA bioconjugates (CDP-M-H3 and CDP-M-H4) were prepared and stored in a similar synthetic process.

Each cleaned GCE was immersed in 1% HAuCl4 solution and electrodeposited at −0.2 V for 10 s to obtain gold nanoparticle-modified GCE (Au/GCE). After cleaving the SS bonds by Tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride, 2 μM SH-CP was incubated on Au/GCE overnight (SH-CP/Au/GCE) and then blocked with 6-mercaptohexanol (MCH, 1 mM) for 30 min (MCH/SH-CP/Au/GCE). Meanwhile, the CHA cycle was initiated in a mixture containing 10 μL of H1 (10 μM), 10 μL of H2 (10 μM), 75 μL of Tris−HCl buffer and miRNA-21 at different concentrations. After reaction for 80 min at 37 °C, 20 μL of mixture was dropped onto the blocked electrode and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C (CHA/MCH/SH-CP/Au/GCE). Next, 10 μL of a mixture of o-CDP-M-H3 and o-CDP-M-H4 was dropped and incubated for another 80 min to complete the HCR reaction (HCR/CHA/MCH/SH-CP/Au/GCE). After each step, the modified electrode was rinsed with Tris−HCl to remove the physical adsorption. The fabrication process of the biosensor is shown in Scheme 1 . In addition, the biosensor prepared using CDP-M as the luminophore with a similar process was constructed as a control.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of CDP-M and o-CDP-M show that the morphology is well dispersed without significant aggregation (Fig. 1 A and B). The average diameters of CDP-M and o-CDP-M are approximately 3.2 nm and 3.9 nm, respectively (lower inset). Well-resolved lattice spacings of approximately 0.21 nm and 0.19 nm are observed in CDP-M and o-CDP-M, respectively (upper inset), which are consistent with the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene [25]. The chemical compositions of the prepared CDP-M and o-CDP-M were investigated with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 A and B show that the characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s all existed in CDP-M and o-CDP-M. Elemental analysis revealed that 64.31 % carbon and 22.98 % oxygen were found in CDP-M, whereas 51.96 % carbon and 34.32 % oxygen were present in o-CDP-M. Compared with CDP-M, the increase in oxygen content of o-CDP-M indicates that CDP-M had been oxidized by H2O2. The expanded peaks of C 1s in CDP-M and o-CDP-M are similar, and they show that four peaks are related to CC/C=C, C-N, C-O and C=O/C=N groups (Fig. 2C and D). The high-resolution N1 s XPS spectra of CDP-M and o-CDP-M indicate the presence of NH, N-(C)3 and C-N-C (Fig. 2E and F). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify the functional groups. As shown in Fig. 1C, no obvious difference in the FTIR spectrum was observed between CDP-M and o-CDP-M, suggesting that the structure of o-CDP-M remained unchanged. In addition, a broad peak of OH groups and -NH groups was located at 3403 cm−1, a sharp absorption of CO groups was observed centred at 1596 cm−1, and an absorption peak of CO stretching vibrations was at approximately 1100 cm−1, implying that abundant carboxyl groups existed. An absorption peak at approximately 1400 cm-1 belonged to the stretching vibration of CN. The carbon types of CDP-M and o-CDP-M were explored via Raman spectrometry. The G-band peaks appeared at approximately 1591 cm−1, and the D-band peaks were located at 1355 cm−1 due to the vibration of defective carbon and sp2 hybridized carbon (Fig. 1D). The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) was used to assess the extent of disorder of carbon-based nanomaterials. The obtained ID/IG of CDP-M and o-CDP-M were 0.72 and 1.66, respectively, revealing that more surface defective structures existed in o-CDP-M than in CDP-M, which may have led to stronger ECL performance [26,27].

Furthermore, the optical properties were investigated by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL). Fig. 1E illustrates the UV–vis absorption band with a peak at approximately 295 nm of CDP-M and o-CDP-M, which was generated by the electron transition of nanocarbons [28]. Fig. 1F shows that the FL emission of CDP-M was at 439.4 nm, while that of o-CDP-M was redshifted to 473.6 nm. The FL redshift also occurred in o-CDP-F and o-CDP-B (Fig. S1). This phenomenon was attributed to the surface traps by the loose shell causing luminescence at longer wavelengths [29]. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of o-CDP-M was investigated by integrating sphere measurements, and the result was 44.87 % (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL properties of CDPs and o-CDPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3 A shows the ECL behaviours of CDPs and o-CDPs prepared from different leaves in 50 mM S2O8 2−. A weak ECL response (518 a.u.) appeared on a bare GCE (curve d), while the ECL intensities of CDP-M/GCE, CDP-F/GCE and CDP-B/GCE were 2895, 3143 and 1367 a.u. (curve a to c), respectively. From Fig. 3B, the ECL intensities of o-CDP-M/GCE, o-CDP-F/GCE and o-CDP-B/GCE were correspondingly improved to 5550, 3998 and 3263 a.u. (curve a to c), respectively. Obviously, the ECL intensities were greatly enhanced by oxidation, and the highest ECL signal was observed on o-CDP-M/GCE. The reason for this observation was that o-CDPs with more defective states and more “loose shells” with oxygen-containing groups facilitated the electrogeneration of o-CDPs
– radicals. Subsequently, more SO4
– could be generated easily by S2O8 2− to produce a stronger ECL response [30]. The possible ECL mechanism of the o-CDPs/S2O8 2− system is proposed as follows:
(1)

(2)

O 8

e −
→ S
O 4

+ S
O 4

(3)
o − C
D P
s
• −
+ S
O 4
· −
→ o − C
D P
s *
+ S
O 4
2 −
(4)

D P
s *
→ o − C
D pS
+ h v

The ECL intensities of the o-CDP-M/S2O8 2− system under 16 consecutive scan cycles with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.7 % (Fig. 3C) hinted at the desired stability. The 3D ECL maximum emission wavelength of the o-CDP-M/S2O8 2− system was 520 nm (Fig. 3D). Compared with the FL spectra (Fig. 1F b), it had a redshift of 46.4 nm since the ECL emission hinged more on the surface properties [31]. From Fig. S5, stable ECL signals were acquired on CDP-M/GCE, CDP-F/GCE, CDP-B/GCE, o-CDP-F/GCE and o-CDP-B/GCE with S2O8 2- as the coreactant, indicating that CDPs and o-CDPs have the potential to become ECL luminophores. Among them, o-CDP-M had the best performance as an optimal ECL luminophore.

With the help of the cascade amplification strategy, o-CDP-M was employed as a new type of “green source” luminophore to develop a “signal-on” ECL biosensor. MiRNA-21 was selected as a model target to verify the practicability of the designed ECL assay. All hairpin DNA was skilfully designed to dynamically prevent any spontaneous interaction without targeting. The CHA cycle was triggered by target miRNA-21 (Scheme 1B). After H1 hybridized with miRNA-21, the freshly exposed segment of H1 opened the hairpin structure of H2 to form H1 and H2 bioconjugates (H1-H2), while miRNA-21 was released to trigger the next CHA cycle. Then, a free end of H1-H2 hybridized with SH-CP was incubated on Au/GCE via Au-S bonds. Afterwards, H1-H2 acted as the HCR initiator, and its active section opened o-CDP-M-H3. The newly exposed domain of o-CDP-M-H3 unfolded o-CDP-M-H4, and a newly exposed region in H4 in turn opened o-CDP-M-H3 so that o-CDP-M-H4 and o-CDP-M-H3 could hybridize alternately on the electrode. Thus, dsDNA polymers were produced, o-CDP-M was introduced into the electrode, and a “signal-on” state was achieved. With the increase in the dose of miRNA-21, more dsDNA polymers were formed, introducing more o-CDP-M through cascade amplification, which realized an increase in the ECL signal to determine miRNA-21.

The feasibility of CHA and HCR amplification was verified by natural polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE, 16 %). MiRNA-21, H1, H2, H3 and H4 corresponded to a clear single band with different electrophoretic mobilities (lanes 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6, Fig. 4 A). Subsequently, two clear bands were observed in miRNA-21+H1 + H2 (lane 4), one band with lower mobility indicating assembly of the CHA cycle and the other band with higher mobility suggesting a surplus of H1 and H2. Moreover, inhomogeneous ladder-shaped large bands appeared (lane 7) after the addition of H3 and H4 into the CHA product. The nonspecific reaction between different hairpin DNA samples was also investigated by PAGE (Fig. 4B). H1, H2, H3 and H4 were clearly associated with a single band with different electrophoretic mobilities (lanes 1, 2, 4 and 5). The mixture of H1 and H2 (H1 + H2) exhibited two independent bands (lane 3), demonstrating nonexistent dynamic spontaneous interaction between H1 and H2. A large band in lane 6 had the same electrophoretic mobility as the bands in lanes 4 and 5, showing no dynamic spontaneous interaction between H3 and H4. Moreover, three bright bands (lane 7) showed that H1, H2, H3 and H4 had no dynamic spontaneous interaction. As expected, the feasibility of CHA and HCR amplification was verified. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to characterize the stepwise-modified electrode. As shown in Fig. 4C, CV shows a distinct redox peak of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- on bare GCE (curve a). After Au NPs were modified on the electrode, an increased peak current was observed (curve b). With the introduction of SH-CP and MCH step by step, the peak currents decreased gradually (curves c and d) because the diffusion of ferricyanide to the electrode surface was hindered by SH-CP and MCH. Afterwards, H1-H2 formed by CHA assembled onto the electrode, and a further decreased redox current appeared (curve e) due to bioconjugates restricting electron transfer. Finally, with the incubation of the mixture of o-CDP-M-H3 and o-CDP-M-H4, a lower redox current could be observed (curve f). The EIS results (Fig. 4D) were consistent with the CV results, indicating the successful construction of the CHA/HCR amplification sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the proposed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions (Fig. S6), with the help of CHA/HCR cascade amplification, the sensing platforms using o-CDP-M and CDP-M as luminophores were comparatively studied for the detection of miRNA-21. As depicted in Fig. 5 A and C, the ECL intensities obtained from o-CDP-M gradually increased with increasing concentrations of miRNA-21, showing dynamic ranges from 1 fM to 1 nM o-CDP-M and from 10 fM to 1 nM CDP-M (Fig. 5B and D, respectively). The regression equations are I (o-CDP-M) = 1.17 × 103 lg c + 18.90 × 103 (R2 = 0.996) and I (CDP-M) = 0.75 × 103 lg c + 11.41 × 103 (R2 = 0.993), where I and c represent the ECL intensity and miRNA-21 concentration. The limits of detection (LODs) were 0.27 fM o-CDP-M and 7.49 fM CDP-M (S/N = 3). The above results show that the biosensor with o-CDP-M as the ECL luminophore had a better analytical performance and a lower detection limit, which was inseparable from the higher signal of o-CDP-M with more defective states and oxygen-containing groups. Utilizing o-CDP-M as a high-performance ECL luminophore, the as-proposed biosensor based on o-CDP-M was further investigated. Compared with other reported methods (Table S1), the proposed biosensor presents a higher sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the proposed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity are the fundamental elements that reflect the value of ECL biosensors. The sensor was analysed by consecutive cyclic scanning with miRNA-21 (10 pM) for 16 cycles. No obvious change in ECL intensity was observed (RSD = 2.2 %), indicating satisfactory stability (Fig. 6 A). Under the same conditions, five different electrodes were used to test the same concentration of miRNA-21 (10 pM) with an RSD of 3.5 %, revealing acceptable reproducibility (Fig. 6B). The interferences of other miRNAs (10 pM), including miRNA-155, miRNA-182-5p, miRNA-126, and the mixture (1 pM miRNA-21 + 10 pM interfering substances), were investigated (Fig. 6C). The ECL responses of interfering miRNAs were almost the same as those of the blank. Moreover, despite the presence of a large excess of other interferences in the mixture (10-fold), no noticeable ECL difference was obtained compared with miRNA-21, hinting at the high selectivity.

To evaluate the performance of the biosensor on real samples, miRNA-21 in lysates of human breast (MCF-7) and cervical (HeLa) cancer cells was monitored. Cell sample lysates were obtained from commercial microRNA extraction kits. Fig. 6D shows that the ECL responses significantly increased with increasing concentrations of MCF-7 cells from 101 to 104, proving high expression of miRNA-21 in MCF-7 cells. However, the ECL intensities slightly changed with increasing concentrations of HeLa cells from 101 to 104, suggesting low expression of miRNA-21 in HeLa cells. These phenomena are consistent with previous reports [32]. Hence, the constructed biosensor has the potential to detect miRNA-21 in cancer cells.

In summary, CDPs and o-CDPs were synthesized by simple pyrolysis from a "green source". Among them, o-CDP-M showed excellent ECL performance due to abundant oxygen-containing groups. With the help of a cascade amplification strategy, o-CDP-M as a luminophore was used to fabricate a “signal-on” biosensor, which achieved ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-21 from 1 fM to 1 nM with a LOD of 0.27 fM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, satisfactory results were obtained in cell sample lysates. This work provides a promising alternative for the application of "green source" materials to develop an efficient ECL platform in biochemical analysis.

Gui Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Qian Han: Conceptualization, Visualization. Li Song: Validation. Pingkun Liu: Software. Guangrong Kuang: Data curation. Yingzi Fu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22077105) and Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0854), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130529.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Chem. 87 (2015) 8578-8583.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Gui Zhang is a MS candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Qian Han is a PhD candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Li Song is a MS candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Pingkun Liu is a MS candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Guangrong Kuang is a MS candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.

Yingzi Fu is a professor of chemistry at Southwest University, China. She received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest University in 2006. Her research focuses on chemical sensors and biosensors.
